goal_title_suggestions: Trade My Plan Every Single Day
Build Iron Discipline in My Trading

variants: Open a blank document right now and write the header 'My Trading Rules' at the top — nothing else yet, just open it and save it.
Create a new file called 'My Trading Rules' in your journal tool of choice (Notion, Google Sheets, or a plain doc) and save it with today's date.
In Notion, Google Docs, or a spreadsheet, create a doc titled 'My One Setup' and leave it open on your screen.
tasks.why: The journal is your primary discipline tool — it starts here.

variants: Write exactly 2 sentences describing the ONE setup you trade most often — what has to be true on the chart before you enter.
In your new doc, write your go-to entry condition in plain English: what pattern, level, or signal triggers you to take a trade.
Define your primary setup in writing: one sentence for what the chart must show, one sentence for what confirms the entry — no more.
tasks.why: You can't audit discipline without a written rule to audit against.

variants: Add three more fields below your entry rule: 'Stop Loss Rule,' 'Exit/Target Rule,' and 'Max Loss Per Trade' — fill in each one with a specific number or condition, not a vague answer.
Below your entry condition, write your exact stop placement rule, your profit target rule, and your max dollar risk per trade — be specific, no ranges.
Complete your setup doc with three lines: where your stop goes (exact rule), where you exit at target (exact rule), and what % of your account you risk per trade (one number).
tasks.why: A full written setup gives you something concrete to follow — and to violate.

variants: Pull up your last 10 closed trades in your brokerage and export or screenshot the list — get them in front of you right now.
Open your brokerage's trade history and find your 10 most recent closed trades — write the ticker and result (win/loss/break-even) for each.
Log into your brokerage, navigate to trade history, and list your last 10 trades by ticker, date, and P&L — copy them into your journal doc.
tasks.why: The audit only works with real data in front of you.

variants: For each of the 10 trades, write 'IN-PLAN' or 'IMPULSE' next to it — a trade is in-plan only if it matched your written setup from Day 1 exactly.
Label every trade from your list: did it match your Day 1 entry rule exactly? Write IN-PLAN or IMPULSE next to each one, no in-between.
Go trade by trade: compare each one to your setup rules from yesterday — mark it IN-PLAN or IMPULSE based only on your written entry rule, not on outcome.
tasks.why: Separating plan from impulse is the first act of real discipline.

variants: Count your IN-PLAN vs IMPULSE trades, calculate the win rate for each group separately, and write both numbers in your doc — face the data.
Tally up: how many IN-PLAN trades did you win vs lose? Same for IMPULSE — write the win rate for each group side by side in your journal.
In your doc, write four numbers: IN-PLAN wins, IN-PLAN losses, IMPULSE wins, IMPULSE losses — then calculate the win % for each group.
tasks.why: Most traders find impulse trades hurt more than they help — the numbers will show it.

variants: Open TradingView and pull up one of your recent IN-PLAN winning trades — find the chart for that ticker on that day.
Find the chart of your best IN-PLAN trade from the last 10 — open TradingView, enter the ticker, set the timeframe you trade.
On TradingView, load the chart for your clearest IN-PLAN winner from your Day 2 audit — get it on screen.
tasks.why: Seeing a clean execution visually reinforces what right looks like.

variants: Mark the exact entry point, stop level, and exit point on the chart using TradingView's drawing tools — save it as a screenshot.
Draw a horizontal line at your entry, a line at your stop, and a line at your target on the chart — screenshot and save it to your journal folder.
Use TradingView annotations to mark entry, stop, and target on your best IN-PLAN trade's chart — save the image with a filename like 'Day3-clean-setup.png.'
tasks.why: A visual reference of a clean trade is a coaching tool you'll return to.

variants: Now do the same for your clearest IMPULSE loss: find the chart, mark where you entered vs where your rules said to enter, and write one sentence on why you deviated.
Pull up your worst IMPULSE trade's chart, mark the actual entry vs where your setup rule would have placed it, and write exactly what triggered you to jump in early.
Chart your most obvious IMPULSE trade: draw the actual entry, draw where your rule said to wait, and write one sentence in your doc explaining the mental trigger that caused the deviation.
tasks.why: Seeing the deviation on a chart makes the pattern undeniable.

variants: Open your journal doc and add a 'Risk Per Trade' section — write your max risk as a percentage of account (1% or 2%) and convert it to a dollar amount based on your current balance.
In your trading rules doc, add one line: 'Max risk per trade = X%' and below it write the current dollar value that equals — calculate it right now.
Add a 'Position Sizing' row to your journal and fill in: account size, max risk %, and the exact dollar amount you will not exceed per trade.
tasks.why: Discipline without a hard dollar limit is just intention.

variants: For your next planned trade, calculate position size using your stop distance and your max dollar risk — write the math in your journal before you place the order.
Pick any ticker you're watching and run a position size calculation: (max dollar risk) / (entry price - stop price) = shares — write this out in your doc.
Practice the position sizing formula on a hypothetical trade: set a $50 max risk, choose an entry and stop level, and calculate exactly how many shares that allows — log it.
tasks.why: Sizing math done before entry prevents oversized emotional positions.

variants: Go back to your last 10 trades and calculate what your actual risk was on each one — note which trades exceeded your max % rule and by how much.
For each of the 10 trades in your Day 2 audit, figure out the dollar amount you had at risk (shares × stop distance) and flag any that exceeded your new max rule.
Audit your last 10 trades for position sizing: compute the actual risk taken on each trade and mark 'OVER' or 'OK' — count how many violated your new risk limit.
tasks.why: Oversizing is one of the most common ways discipline breaks down.

variants: Set up your trade journal template right now: create a row or entry format with the fields Ticker, Date, Setup, Entry, Stop, Target, Shares, Result, In-Plan Y/N — save the template.
Build your journal entry template: add columns for Date, Ticker, Entry Price, Stop Price, Target, Size, P&L, and In-Plan tag — one row per trade going forward.
Create your standard trade log format with at minimum these 7 fields: Date, Ticker, Entry, Stop, Target, Position Size, and Plan-Adherence — set it up now.
tasks.why: A consistent log format forces complete thinking before and after every trade.

variants: Log your 3 most recent trades into the new journal template retroactively — pull the data from your brokerage and fill in every field, including whether each was in-plan.
Backfill your last 3 trades into the new template: go to your brokerage history, get the exact entry/exit prices, and fill in all fields including the Plan-Adherence tag.
Enter your 3 most recent trades into the journal format you just built — use real numbers from your brokerage, not estimates, and tag each one IN-PLAN or IMPULSE.
tasks.why: Backfilling forces you to confront real numbers, not memory.

variants: Write a one-paragraph 'Current State Assessment' in your journal: what is your biggest discipline failure right now — entering too early, exiting too early, oversizing, or chasing — be specific.
In your journal, write 3-5 sentences answering this exact question: what is the single most common way you deviate from your plan, and what does it feel like in the moment right before you do it.

Add a 'Discipline Diagnosis' section to your doc and write your honest answer: which of these describes you most — entering before confirmation, moving stops, sizing up on conviction, or revenge trading after a loss.
tasks.why: Naming your specific failure pattern is the first step to interrupting it.

variants: Open TradingView and add a watchlist called 'My Setup Candidates' — scan for 3 tickers that are currently setting up near a level your entry rule requires.
Build a 'Watchlist' section in your journal and list 3 tickers right now that are forming the setup you defined on Day 1 — include the key level to watch.
Identify 3 stocks or futures contracts that are approaching the type of setup in your Day 1 entry rule — list them in your journal with the specific price level to watch.
tasks.why: Pre-market preparation replaces reactive scanning during market hours.

variants: For each of the 3 watchlist tickers, write the exact entry trigger, stop price, and target price you would use if the setup triggers — no entry without all three defined.
On each of your 3 setup candidates, define: what has to happen for you to enter, where your stop goes, and what your target is — write all three for all three tickers.
Complete a pre-trade plan for each watchlist ticker: trigger condition, stop level, profit target — if you can't fill in all three, remove that ticker from the list.
tasks.why: Writing the plan before the market opens removes in-the-moment decision pressure.

variants: On TradingView, mark your entry zone, stop level, and target on a chart for each of the 3 tickers — screenshot each chart and attach to your journal entry for today.
Draw horizontal lines for entry, stop, and target on each of your 3 watchlist charts in TradingView — save screenshots as 'Day6-[TICKER]-preplan.png' for each.
Annotate all 3 watchlist charts: entry level, stop line, and target line on each — take a screenshot and paste it into today's journal entry before the open.
tasks.why: Marked charts act as a commitment device — you can see if you followed the plan after the fact.

variants: Pull up your journal from Days 1-6 and read everything you've written — setup rules, audit results, discipline diagnosis — all of it before adding anything new.
Open your trading rules doc and journal, read from Day 1 through yesterday, and note anything that feels incomplete or wrong — just read first.
Review your full journal from this week: setup definition, audit numbers, risk rules, watchlist format — read it like a coach reviewing a player's notes.
tasks.why: Week-one review anchors the rules before real trading begins.

variants: Write a '7-Day Audit Summary' in your journal: how many IN-PLAN vs IMPULSE trades in your last 10, what your average risk was vs your rule, and your #1 identified discipline failure.

Create a Week 1 Summary entry with three bullets: (1) your plan-adherence rate from the audit, (2) your average actual risk % vs your rule, (3) the specific moment in your trading where discipline breaks down.
Add a Week 1 Stats block to your journal: IN-PLAN %, IMPULSE %, average position size vs rule, and a one-sentence diagnosis of your weakest habit.
tasks.why: Quantifying your starting point gives you something to beat next week.

variants: Write your Week 2 commitment in your journal: max trades per day, max risk per trade, and one specific behavior you will do differently this week based on your diagnosis — commit to numbers, not vibes.

In your journal, write a binding commitment for next week: 'I will take a maximum of X trades per day, risk no more than Y% per trade, and I will [specific behavior change] when I feel the urge to deviate.'

Close out Week 1 by writing three rules you will follow with zero exceptions next week: your trade limit per day, your max risk per trade, and one specific if-then rule for your biggest discipline failure.
tasks.why: A pre-committed week plan beats willpower in the moment.

variants: Before the market opens, write today's pre-market plan: which tickers you're watching, what your entry trigger is for each, and your hard stop for calling it a day (max 2 trades or max loss).
Open your journal and write today's trade plan right now: 3 or fewer tickers to watch, the specific trigger for each entry, and today's maximum trade count.
Write a pre-session plan in your journal: list the setups you're watching, define the trigger for each, and set a hard daily trade limit — then close your journal and trade only that plan.
tasks.why: Pre-market planning is what separates professional traders from reactive ones.

variants: After the session, log any trades taken into your journal template — every field, no skipping — and mark each one IN-PLAN or IMPULSE.
Complete your post-session journal entry: fill in all fields for every trade you took today, tag each as IN-PLAN or IMPULSE, and note the actual vs planned entry price.
Log today's trades into the journal immediately after the session: entry, exit, size, result, and whether it matched your pre-market plan — no incomplete rows.
tasks.why: Logging same-day while memory is fresh gives accurate data.

variants: For any IMPULSE trade you took today, write a 3-sentence post-mortem: what triggered the impulse, what the chart actually looked like vs your rules, and what you will do differently tomorrow.

If you took any out-of-plan trades today, write in your journal: the exact thought or feeling that led to the entry, whether the chart supported your rules, and the specific action you'll take to prevent it tomorrow.

Write an IMPULSE trade explanation for every off-plan trade from today's session: what you saw that made you enter, what your rules actually required, and your exact plan for handling that same signal tomorrow.
tasks.why: Written post-mortems on impulse trades interrupt the repeat cycle.

variants: Write today's pre-market plan in your journal before doing anything else — tickers, triggers, hard stop — same format as yesterday.
Open your journal and complete today's pre-session plan: what you're watching, what has to happen for you to enter, and your max trade count for the day.
Before looking at the market, write your plan: 3 or fewer tickers, entry trigger for each, and what will end your trading day early (loss limit or trade count).
tasks.why: The pre-plan habit is built by doing it the same way every day.

variants: After today's session, pull up your Day 8 post-session notes and compare: did the same impulse pattern show up again, or did yesterday's post-mortem change your behavior today?
After trading, open your journal and check yesterday's IMPULSE post-mortem — did you repeat the same mistake today or did your written plan stop it? Write one sentence on the result.
Compare today's behavior to yesterday's: look at your Day 8 post-mortem and note whether the specific trigger you identified caused any issue today — write a one-sentence update.
tasks.why: Tracking whether post-mortems actually change behavior closes the feedback loop.

variants: Log all of today's trades, tag each IN-PLAN or IMPULSE, and calculate your running IN-PLAN % across Days 8-9 — write the number in your journal.
Complete today's post-session log, then calculate: out of all trades taken since Day 8, what percentage were IN-PLAN — write that number and whether it's better or worse than your Week 1 audit.
Fill in the full journal row for each trade today, then compute your in-plan adherence rate for the past 2 days combined — add it to a running stats table in your journal.
tasks.why: Tracking the metric in real time is what makes the number move.

variants: Write your pre-market plan before opening any charts — tickers, triggers, trade limit — same format, same habit.
Start today with your journal open: write the plan first, then open the market — tickers, entry conditions, max trades.
Pre-market plan first, market second — write down which setups you're watching and what triggers an entry before you look at price action.
tasks.why: Consistency in the routine builds the discipline muscle.

variants: Review the 3 annotated charts you saved on Day 6 — compare those pre-planned setups to how they actually played out and write one observation per ticker.
Pull up the Day 6 pre-plan screenshots of your 3 watchlist tickers — look at what actually happened after you drew those levels and write one sentence per chart on what you learned.
Open the TradingView charts from your Day 6 watchlist and see how each setup resolved — write a brief note per ticker on whether the level held, broke, or never triggered.
tasks.why: Reviewing pre-planned charts teaches pattern recognition without bias.

variants: Log today's trades, write your IMPULSE post-mortems if any, and add a 'Streak' line to your journal that tracks consecutive IN-PLAN days — update it now.
Complete your post-session log for today, then add a new row to your journal called 'Plan-Adherence Streak' — count how many consecutive days you've had zero impulse trades and write the number.
After logging today's trades, start tracking a discipline streak: how many trading days in a row have you had zero out-of-plan entries — write the current streak number in your journal.
tasks.why: A streak creates a positive incentive to protect the record.

variants: Write today's pre-market plan and add one line: 'If I feel the urge to enter before my trigger fires, I will [specific action]' — fill in the blank with a real behavior, not 'wait.'
Complete your pre-session plan and this time add an if-then rule: 'If I feel tempted to enter outside my plan, I will [close the platform / write in journal / walk away for 2 minutes].'
Before trading, write your setup plan and your interrupt rule: what specific action you'll take the moment you notice yourself about to take an impulsive trade.
tasks.why: Pre-committed interrupts are more reliable than in-the-moment willpower.

variants: Screen for 3 fresh setup candidates using your entry rule — list them in your journal with the specific trigger price or pattern you need before entry.
Scan for tickers that match your Day 1 setup criteria and add 3 candidates to your watchlist with entry trigger, stop, and target pre-filled.
Find 3 new tickers setting up for your primary entry rule and plan them in full: trigger, stop, target — add them to your journal's watchlist section.
tasks.why: Having a list ready prevents random scanning during market hours.

variants: After the session, log all trades, compute today's IN-PLAN %, and write a one-sentence honest answer to: did your interrupt rule work, and if not, what would have worked better.
Post-session: complete the trade log, check whether your interrupt rule from this morning's plan actually triggered, and write what happened the moment before your hardest decision today.
Log your trades, then evaluate your if-then interrupt rule: did it get activated today, did it work, and write a one-sentence tweak if it didn't hold up.
tasks.why: Testing and refining the interrupt rule is how it becomes reliable.

variants: Pre-market plan — write it before opening charts — add your current discipline streak count at the top of today's entry.
Open your journal, write your pre-session plan, and note your current plan-adherence streak at the top — make it visible before you start trading.
Start your journal entry with your streak number, then write today's plan — your streak is a number worth protecting.
tasks.why: Seeing the streak first thing creates accountability before the session starts.

variants: Pull up your brokerage P&L for the past 10 trading days and separate the results into two columns: IN-PLAN trade days vs days where you took an impulse trade — write the total P&L for each column.
Segment your last 10 trading days by discipline: list each day as 'clean' (all in-plan) or 'deviated' (impulse trade taken) and write the day's P&L next to each.
In your journal, create a 10-day table with three columns: date, clean/deviated, and day P&L — fill it in using your brokerage history and your trade log tags.
tasks.why: Seeing P&L by discipline quality is more motivating than seeing raw P&L.

variants: Write a paragraph in your journal on what you notice in the data: are your clean days more profitable, less profitable, or mixed — and what does that tell you about where your edge actually lives.
Based on the 10-day table you just built, write 3 sentences on the relationship between discipline and P&L in your trading — what pattern do you see and what does it mean for your approach.
Analyze your clean vs deviated day P&L split: write a short journal entry on whether following your plan is making you money, losing you money, or if the data is still unclear — be specific.
tasks.why: Data-driven reflection is more durable than motivation.

variants: Pre-market plan as usual — write tickers, triggers, trade limit — then add one column to your journal template: 'Emotion Tag' (calm / anxious / FOMO / revenge) to fill in after each trade.
Write today's pre-session plan and update your journal template to include an emotion tag for each trade — you'll fill it in right after each trade executes.
Complete your pre-market plan and add 'Emotion at Entry' as a new field in your trade log — one word only: calm, rushed, FOMO, or revenge.
tasks.why: Tracking emotion at entry reveals whether decisions are plan-driven or state-driven.

variants: After each trade today, immediately write your emotion tag before looking at the P&L — force yourself to record the feeling before the outcome is processed.
Fill in your emotion tag right after entry, before checking P&L — do this for every trade today without exception.
The moment a trade closes, write your emotion tag in the journal — one word, before you look at how much you made or lost.
tasks.why: Pre-P&L tagging prevents rationalization of emotional trades.

variants: After the session, look at your emotion tags alongside the IN-PLAN/IMPULSE tags — write one observation about whether emotional state predicted plan adherence.
Post-session: compare your emotion tags to your in-plan tags for today — write a one-sentence observation: were your FOMO or rushed entries more likely to be out-of-plan?
Review today's trade log with emotion tags filled in and write a short note on any pattern: were calm entries more likely to be in-plan and impulsive entries more likely to be off-plan?
tasks.why: The emotion-discipline link becomes visible in the data over time.

variants: Open your full journal from Day 1 to today — read every entry, every note, every number — before writing anything new.
Do a complete 2-week read-through of your journal: setup rules, audit data, trade logs, post-mortems, emotion tags — read it all.
Review your journal from the beginning: Day 1 setup definition through today's entries — read it in full before you start your 2-week summary.
tasks.why: The 2-week review only has value if you've re-read everything first.

variants: Write your 2-Week Review: total trades taken, IN-PLAN %, average risk per trade vs your rule, longest clean streak, and whether your P&L improved on clean days vs deviated days.
Create a '2-Week Stats Summary' in your journal with these 5 numbers: total trades, in-plan rate %, average $ risk per trade, clean-day win rate, and impulse-day win rate.
Fill in your 2-week report card: total trades, plan adherence %, mean position risk vs your rule %, win rate on clean days, win rate on days with impulse trades — be exact.
tasks.why: Two weeks of real data is enough to see patterns — you just have to look.

variants: Write your Phase 2 commitment in your journal: starting tomorrow, you will log every trade same-day, mark every trade IN-PLAN or IMPULSE, write a mandatory post-mortem for any impulse trade, and keep max risk at 1-2% per trade — sign it with today's date.

Write a binding Phase 2 protocol in your journal: daily log (no exceptions), IN-PLAN tag on every trade, written explanation for every impulse trade, max 2 trades per day, max 1-2% risk — treat it as a contract.

Commit to Phase 2 rules in writing: every trade gets logged that day, every off-plan trade gets a written 'why,' you will not exceed 2 trades per session, and you will not exceed your defined risk % — write it down and date it.
tasks.why: Writing the Phase 2 commitment turns intent into a testable standard.

variants: Phase 2 starts today — write your pre-market plan with your new hard limits on screen: max 2 trades, max 1-2% risk per trade, no entry without full pre-plan.
Open your journal and write today's Phase 2 plan: tickers, triggers, max trade count (2), max risk per trade (your defined %) — this is now your daily standard.
Start Day 15 with a written plan that includes your Phase 2 constraints at the top: 2-trade max, defined risk %, entry trigger required — then plan your specific setups.
tasks.why: Phase 2 discipline is about repeating the same standard, not increasing complexity.

variants: After the session, log your trades, compute your risk on each, and flag any that exceeded 1-2% — no excuses, just the number.
Post-session: fill in today's trade log with all fields including actual risk % per trade — highlight any that went over your Phase 2 limit.
Complete your daily log: every trade, every field, including position size and actual risk % — mark any violations of your Phase 2 rules in red or with a flag.
tasks.why: Same-day logging at Phase 2 standards builds the accountability loop.

variants: Write a mandatory explanation in your journal for any trade that was out-of-plan today — if zero impulse trades, write one sentence confirming that and note what helped you stay clean.
For any off-plan trade today: write why you took it, what the chart showed vs your rules, and what you'll do differently — if none, write what specifically helped you stick to the plan.
Post-session impulse audit: for each IMPULSE trade, write a full explanation; if you had a clean day, write a one-sentence note on what kept you disciplined — both are required entries.
tasks.why: Documenting clean days is as important as documenting failures.

variants: Write your pre-market plan before opening any charts — tickers, triggers, 2-trade limit, risk cap — it's a Phase 2 non-negotiable.
Pre-session plan first: which setups are you watching, what are the exact triggers, and what ends your day early — write it before touching the market.
Journal open, plan written, then market open — that's the order from now on, every day.
tasks.why: The routine becomes automatic when it's done the same way every single day.

variants: Screen for 3 fresh candidates that match your setup rules and add them to your watchlist with pre-planned entry, stop, and target for each.
Find 3 tickers that currently match your Day 1 entry setup and plan them fully: trigger price, stop level, target level — add to your watchlist.
Build your watchlist for tomorrow: 3 tickers that fit your entry criteria, each with a full plan written — no tickers on the list without a complete plan.
tasks.why: Pre-planned watchlists eliminate in-session decision fatigue.

variants: Log today's trades, write any IMPULSE explanations, and calculate your running in-plan rate for Phase 2 (Days 15-16) — write the number.
Complete your daily log with all fields, check your Phase 2 risk compliance on each trade, and update your running plan-adherence % for this phase.
Post-session: fill in the trade log, check every trade against Phase 2 rules (2-trade limit, risk cap, in-plan tag), and write your current streak count.
tasks.why: Tracking Phase 2 metrics separately shows whether the new standard is holding.

variants: Pre-market plan, Phase 2 constraints at the top, then your setups — same format, no skipping.
Write your plan before the open: constraints first (2-trade max, risk cap), then which tickers and what triggers today.
Open the journal, write the Phase 2 limits, then write today's specific setup plan — in that order, every time.
tasks.why: Writing the constraints first makes them visible before temptation arises.

variants: Pull up your brokerage's P&L graph for the last 3 weeks and screenshot it — paste it into your journal alongside a one-line note on the trend.
Go to your brokerage's performance section, screenshot the equity curve or P&L chart for the past 15-21 trading days, and add it to your journal.
Export or screenshot your P&L chart from your brokerage covering the last 3 weeks and drop it in your journal — add one sentence describing what the trend line looks like.
tasks.why: Seeing the equity curve regularly connects discipline habits to financial outcome.

variants: Log today's trades, write IMPULSE post-mortems if any, and write a one-paragraph note on what pattern you're seeing in your emotion tags across the last 5 sessions.
Post-session: complete the log, then look at your last 5 days of emotion tags and write one paragraph on whether a specific emotional state keeps showing up before off-plan trades.
Complete your daily log and then do a 5-day emotion audit: look at the tags from Days 13 through today and write what emotional state most often precedes your impulse entries.
tasks.why: The emotion pattern becomes clear after 5 days of tagging.

variants: Pre-market plan written, Phase 2 constraints visible, setups defined — then and only then open the market.
Write the plan: constraints at top, then tickers and triggers, then open your brokerage — that's the sequence.
Before the market opens: journal plan written with Phase 2 limits, setups pre-planned, charts annotated — now you're ready.
tasks.why: The pre-market sequence is a ritual that replaces reactive emotion.

variants: Review the chart you saved on Day 3 — your annotated IN-PLAN winner — and find a current setup that looks structurally similar and add it to today's watchlist.
Open the Day 3 screenshot of your best IN-PLAN trade and look for a current ticker that shows the same structure — add it to your watchlist if it meets your entry criteria.
Pull up your Day 3 annotated chart and scan the market for a setup that mirrors it structurally — list any matches in your watchlist with entry, stop, and target filled in.
tasks.why: Connecting current setups to past winners builds pattern recognition.

variants: After the session, log all trades, calculate today's risk per trade, and write a one-sentence update on whether your interrupt rule (from Day 11) is still working — tweak it if not.
Post-session: fill in the log, check risk compliance, and evaluate your interrupt rule from Day 11 — is it holding up, and if not, what needs to change.
Complete your daily log and revisit your Day 11 interrupt rule: has it prevented any impulse trades this week? Write one sentence on its effectiveness and update it if needed.
tasks.why: Interrupt rules need tuning — checking them keeps them functional.

variants: Pre-market plan written — today also add a 'Max Loss for the Day' line: if you hit this dollar amount, you stop trading, no exceptions.
Write today's plan with one new field: your daily max loss in dollars — the number that, if hit, ends your trading day automatically.
In your pre-session plan, add a hard daily stop-loss dollar amount — write it at the top of today's entry and commit to honoring it.
tasks.why: A daily max loss rule prevents one bad trade from becoming a catastrophic session.

variants: In your journal, write down the last time you had a losing day that felt out of control — what happened, what the P&L was, and whether a daily max loss rule would have stopped the bleeding.
Think of your worst single trading day in the past 3 months: write the total loss, what caused it, and whether stopping earlier would have cut the damage significantly.
Write a 3-sentence entry about your worst losing day: total damage, root cause, and whether a hard daily stop-loss rule would have helped — use real numbers.
tasks.why: Grounding the rule in a real painful memory makes it stick.

variants: Log today's trades, check risk compliance per trade, check whether you stayed under your daily max loss rule, and write one observation about how the new rule changed your behavior.
Post-session log: all fields complete, then check today's total P&L against your new daily max loss — did having the rule visible change how you traded? Write one sentence.
Complete the daily trade log, verify every trade was within risk limits, verify you didn't hit your daily max, and note whether knowing the daily limit made you more careful or less.
tasks.why: The daily max loss rule is most powerful when it changes pre-trade behavior.

variants: Pre-market plan: Phase 2 constraints, daily max loss limit, today's setups — all written before the market opens.
Write the plan: trade limit, risk cap, daily max loss number, specific tickers and triggers — in that order.
Your full pre-session plan includes Phase 2 rules plus the daily max loss line — write it, then trade it.
tasks.why: All three protection rules should be visible before every session.

variants: Build a 'Rules Card' in your journal — a single-page reference containing your entry rule, stop rule, exit rule, max risk %, daily trade limit, and daily max loss — formatted so you can read it in under 30 seconds.
Create a condensed 'Pre-Trade Checklist' in your journal: entry trigger ✓, stop placed ✓, position size calculated ✓, in-plan ✓ — keep it to 5 items or fewer.
Write a reference card you'll open before every trade: your entry rule in one line, stop rule in one line, risk limit in one line, daily trade count in one line — clean and fast to read.
tasks.why: A pre-trade checklist externalizes your rules so you don't rely on memory.

variants: Log today's trades and run through your new Rules Card on each one: did every trade pass all 5 checks before entry? Write yes or no per trade.
Post-session: use your new Pre-Trade Checklist to retroactively score each trade — how many passed all checks? Write the score in your journal.
After the session, evaluate each trade against your Rules Card: did you verify entry rule, stop, and size before entering — write a pass/fail for each trade taken today.
tasks.why: Scoring past trades against the checklist shows where the process breaks down.

variants: Read your full journal from Days 15-20 before writing anything — read every trade log entry, every IMPULSE post-mortem, every emotion tag.
Before adding to your journal, read Phase 2 from the beginning: Day 15 through yesterday — all logs, all notes, all post-mortems.
Open your journal and read Days 15-20 in full: trade data, impulse explanations, emotion tags, rule-card scores — all of it before your 3-week review.
tasks.why: The 3-week review requires seeing Phase 2 as a complete picture.

variants: Write your 3-Week Review: Phase 2 plan-adherence %, average risk per trade, longest clean streak, and whether the daily max loss rule triggered at any point.
Create a 3-Week Stats entry: in-plan rate for Days 15-21, average actual risk %, number of IMPULSE trades and their combined P&L, and current clean streak.
Fill in your 3-week report: plan adherence % for Phase 2, mean risk per trade, total number of impulse trades, impulse trade P&L total, and longest run of clean days.
tasks.why: Three weeks of data shows whether Phase 2 is actually changing behavior.

variants: Write one paragraph on the most important thing the last 3 weeks have shown you about your trading — then write one specific rule update based on that insight.
Based on 3 weeks of data, write what you now know that you didn't know on Day 1 — then update any rule in your trading doc that needs to change based on that knowledge.
3-week reflection: write one thing the data proved about your trading that surprised you, and one specific change you're making to your written rules as a result.
tasks.why: Updating rules based on evidence is how a trading system improves.

variants: Pre-market plan with the updated rules from your 3-week review applied — any rule changes from yesterday go into effect today.
Write today's plan incorporating any rule updates from your Day 21 review — new limits, updated triggers, or refined entry conditions apply starting now.
Apply your 3-week review insights to today's plan: if you updated any rules yesterday, they're live today — write the plan with the new version.
tasks.why: Rules only matter when they're actually applied.

variants: Screen for 3 setup candidates using your updated entry criteria and add them to your watchlist with full plans.
Find 3 tickers that match your (possibly updated) entry rule and write a full plan for each: trigger, stop, target.
Build today's watchlist with 3 pre-planned setups based on your current written entry rule — any rule changes from Week 3 review apply here.
tasks.why: Using updated rules on real setups tests whether the update is workable.

variants: Log today's trades, tag them, compute risk compliance, and write one sentence on how the updated rules felt to trade — easier, harder, or the same.
Post-session: complete the trade log, then write one observation on whether the rule update from Day 21 made today's trading cleaner or created new friction.
Fill in the daily log, check Phase 2 compliance, and write a brief note on what it was like trading with your updated rules for the first time.
tasks.why: Immediate feedback on rule changes prevents bad updates from persisting.

variants: Pre-market plan written — this time, before planning setups, write one sentence answering: what is the most likely way I will deviate from my plan today, and what will I do when that moment comes.
Write your plan and add a pre-commitment line: 'The most likely discipline failure I'll face today is ___, and when it happens I will ___.'
Start your pre-session plan with your daily vulnerability: what specific situation is most likely to pull you off-plan today, and what's your response plan.
tasks.why: Anticipating failure points before they happen reduces reactive decision-making.

variants: Find your best IN-PLAN trade from the past week, pull up its chart, and annotate it as a reference setup — save it as 'Reference-Setup-[date].png' in your journal folder.
Identify the cleanest execution you've made in Phase 2 so far, mark the entry/stop/target on its TradingView chart, and save it as a new reference example.
Pick your most textbook-perfect in-plan trade from the last week, annotate the chart in TradingView, and save it as a reference image alongside your Day 3 example.
tasks.why: Building a reference library of clean executions reinforces what right looks like.

variants: Log today's trades, fill in emotion tags, check the vulnerability you noted this morning — did it come up, and did your pre-commitment hold? Write the answer.
Post-session: complete the log, then report back on your pre-commitment from this morning — did the vulnerability arise, and what actually happened when it did.
Fill in today's trade log, then write a one-paragraph assessment of your morning pre-commitment: did the anticipated failure scenario appear today, and how did you handle it.
tasks.why: Closing the loop on pre-commitments teaches which strategies actually work.

variants: Pre-market plan, vulnerability line, Phase 2 constraints — write it all before touching the market.
Write your complete pre-session plan including the vulnerability pre-commitment — it's part of your standard process now.
Journal open: Phase 2 rules, today's vulnerability, today's setups — in that order, before the open.
tasks.why: The vulnerability line is now a permanent part of pre-session planning.

variants: Go to your trade journal and calculate your average winning trade size vs your average losing trade size over Phase 2 — write the ratio in your journal.
Calculate your reward-to-risk ratio for Phase 2 trades: average winning $ / average losing $ — write the number and compare it to your target ratio from your rules.
In your journal, compute the average P&L of your winning trades and average P&L of your losing trades for Days 15-24 — divide winners by losers to get your actual R:R ratio.
tasks.why: R:R ratio tells you whether your exits are disciplined or premature.

variants: Log today's trades and write a one-paragraph reflection: are you exiting winners too early or holding losers too long — use your actual Phase 2 trade data to answer.
Post-session: complete the log, then write 3 sentences comparing how quickly you exit winning trades vs how long you hold losing ones — be specific with trade examples.

After logging today's trades, write a brief journal note on your exit discipline: are you hitting your planned targets, cutting losers at stop, or doing something else — cite specific Phase 2 examples.
tasks.why: Exit discipline is where many traders give back their edge.

variants: Pre-market plan with full Phase 2 protocol — today, add one line: 'I will let my target be hit before exiting, unless the stop is reached.'
Write your plan and add an exit discipline rule: you do not exit a winning trade early unless a new signal in your written rules says to — write what that looks like.
In today's pre-session plan, write your exit rule explicitly: 'I exit at target or stop, nothing in between, unless [your specific plan allows early exit under these conditions].'
tasks.why: Making exit rules explicit prevents emotion-driven early exits.

variants: Scan for 3 setup candidates and for each one, write your expected R:R ratio based on entry price, stop distance, and target — only include setups where expected R:R is at least 1.5:1.
Find 3 watchlist tickers and calculate R:R for each: (target - entry) / (entry - stop) — add them to your list only if R:R is 1.5 or better.
Build your watchlist using R:R as a filter: only include setups where your planned target is at least 1.5x your stop distance — calculate and write the ratio for each.
tasks.why: Filtering by minimum R:R ensures the math is in your favor before you enter.

variants: Log today's trades with your new exit discipline rule applied and write whether you honored your planned exits — for any early exit, write exactly what triggered it.
Post-session: complete the trade log, evaluate every exit: did it hit the planned target, the stop, or something in between — write a one-sentence explanation for each in-between exit.
Fill in the daily log and grade your exit discipline today: for each trade, write 'TARGET,' 'STOP,' or 'EARLY' in the exit column and explain any EARLY exits in one sentence.
tasks.why: Exit tagging creates the same accountability loop as the entry IN-PLAN tag.

variants: Pre-market plan with the exit discipline rule included — write today's setups with explicit targets and stops listed before the open.
Write your pre-session plan: Phase 2 constraints, vulnerability line, exit rule, and today's specific setups with entry/stop/target for each.
Complete your full pre-session plan: Phase 2 limits, today's setups, and confirm your exit rule is written — trade nothing without a defined target.
tasks.why: Defined targets before entry are as important as defined stops.

variants: Look at the 3 annotated watchlist charts you saved on Day 6 and compare those planned levels to where price actually went — write what you can learn about your target-setting.
Pull up your Day 6 charts and overlay where price actually went after those planned setups — were your targets realistic, too aggressive, or too conservative? Write one sentence per chart.
Review your Day 6 pre-planned trades and check where price eventually went vs your targets — use this to evaluate whether your target levels reflect the actual setup structure.
tasks.why: Reviewing past targets against actual price movement calibrates your target-setting.

variants: Log today's trades with exit tags (TARGET / STOP / EARLY), calculate your exit discipline rate for Phase 2 so far, and write the percentage in your journal.
Post-session: complete the trade log, add exit tags to today's trades, and compute what % of your Phase 2 trades hit their planned exit (target or stop) vs were closed early.
After logging today's trades with exit tags, calculate: out of all Phase 2 trades so far, what % hit planned exits and what % were closed early or moved — write the split.
tasks.why: Quantifying exit discipline creates a metric you can improve.

variants: Pre-market plan, full protocol — today's vulnerability is: what could make you exit a winner early before it hits target? Write that specific scenario and your planned response.
Write your pre-session plan and set your vulnerability line around exit discipline: what's most likely to make you close a winning trade before it reaches target today.
In your pre-session plan, anticipate today's exit challenge: what market condition or emotional state is most likely to cause you to exit at a sub-optimal level — and what will you do instead.
tasks.why: Anticipating exit failures is the next layer of pre-commitment.

variants: Run a paper trade today alongside your real trades: identify a setup that meets your rules but that you didn't take, and track it in your journal as if you had — entry, stop, target, result.
Pick one setup you passed on today and paper trade it: write the entry price, stop, target, and track the outcome — log it separately in your journal as 'Paper Trade.'
Paper-track one setup you decided not to take today: log the entry, stop, and target, then check later where price went and record the result — mark it 'Paper' in your journal.
tasks.why: Paper trading passed setups shows whether you're over-filtering or correctly selective.

variants: Log your real trades and your paper trade for today, compare the outcomes, and write one sentence on what you learned about your real trade selection process.
Post-session: fill in both your real trade log and your paper trade result, then write a brief observation on whether you made the right call to pass on the paper trade or take your real ones.
Complete your trade log (real trades + paper trade) and write one paragraph on what today's comparison taught you about how you select which setups to actually enter.
tasks.why: Comparing real vs paper decisions reveals selectivity biases.

variants: Read your full Phase 2 journal from Day 15 through yesterday — every log entry, every post-mortem, every note — before writing your 4-week review.
Open your journal and read all of Phase 2 before adding anything new: trade logs, vulnerability lines, emotion tags, exit tags, post-mortems — full read.
Before your 4-week review, read Days 15-27 in full — all entries, no skipping — then start writing.
tasks.why: A review is only as good as the data you recall when writing it.

variants: Write your 4-Week Review: plan-adherence % for Phase 2, exit discipline % (planned exit vs early exit), average R:R achieved vs planned, and your current clean-day streak.
Create your 4-week report with these metrics: in-plan trade rate, exit discipline rate, average realized R:R, best clean streak, and whether the daily max loss rule ever triggered.
4-Week Stats Summary: in-plan %, exit discipline %, average R:R achieved, impulse trade count, and total Phase 2 P&L — write all 5 numbers with context.
tasks.why: Two weeks of Phase 2 data shows whether the discipline systems are working.

variants: Write your 4-week commitment: what specific metric will you focus on improving in the next 2 weeks, what rule will you tighten, and what is the specific number you want to hit.
Based on your 4-week data, write one priority for Days 29-42: the single metric most in need of improvement, the specific rule change you'll make, and the target number.
Close Week 4 with a written priority: identify the weakest metric from your Phase 2 stats, write what you'll do differently to improve it, and commit to a specific target number.
tasks.why: Weekly commitments grounded in data create a performance improvement loop.

variants: Pre-market plan with the priority from your 4-week review in focus — write your setups and flag the specific metric you're targeting to improve today.
Open your journal, write today's plan, and at the top note the priority metric from your Day 28 review — it should be visible before every trade.
Write your pre-session plan with your Week 5 focus metric written at the top: the one number you're actively working to improve.
tasks.why: Keeping the priority metric visible keeps improvement intentional.

variants: Screen for 3 setup candidates with your full filter: entry rule match, R:R minimum of 1.5:1, and a pre-defined entry trigger — exclude anything that doesn't meet all three.
Build today's watchlist with a triple filter: setup matches your rules, R:R ≥ 1.5:1, entry trigger is specific not vague — if it doesn't pass all three, it doesn't go on the list.

Find 3 tickers for your watchlist and apply three quality filters: does it match your entry criteria, does the R:R pencil out at 1.5 or better, and is the trigger condition specific — all three required.
tasks.why: Triple-filtering the watchlist raises the quality standard before entry.

variants: Log today's trades, track your priority metric, and write a one-sentence update on whether you made progress on it relative to your recent Phase 2 average.
Post-session: complete the daily log, then check your priority metric for today — better, worse, or same as your Phase 2 average? Write the update.
Fill in the trade log and evaluate your focus metric for today's session: compute it, compare it to your Phase 2 baseline, and write one sentence on the result.
tasks.why: Daily measurement of a priority metric is what moves it.

variants: Write your 30-Day Milestone entry: pull your current plan-adherence %, exit discipline %, average R:R, and clean-day streak — write all four numbers and compare them to where you were on Day 1.
Day 30 checkpoint: write your four key metrics (in-plan %, exit discipline %, realized R:R, clean streak) and note the delta from your pre-Phase 2 audit on Day 2.
30-day progress check: record your current in-plan rate, exit discipline rate, R:R ratio, and longest clean streak — then write a one-sentence comparison to your starting point.
tasks.why: Day 30 is the first real milestone — the numbers tell the story.

variants: Write a 30-day honest assessment: in three paragraphs, describe (1) what discipline system is actually working, (2) what is still breaking down, and (3) what your one setup looks like after 30 days of scrutiny.
In your journal, write 3 paragraphs for the 30-day milestone: what's improved, what's still broken, and whether your written setup from Day 1 still reflects how you actually trade.

30-day journal entry with three sections: (1) what's better since Day 1, (2) what's the same or worse, and (3) whether your Day 1 setup definition needs to be updated based on 30 days of real trading.
tasks.why: Honest 30-day self-assessment is more valuable than a motivational recap.

variants: Write your Phase 3 intent for Days 31-60: you will add a weekly P&L review ritual, you will identify your best-performing setup variation, and you will start narrowing your trades to that one — write what 'best setup' means to you quantitatively.

Define your Days 31-60 focus in writing: weekly P&L reviews, setup performance tracking by variant, and trading only the setup variation with the best historical results — write what criteria you'll use to identify the winner.

Commit to Phase 3 in your journal: weekly performance reviews start next week, you will track which setup variation performs best, and by Day 45 you will have a data-driven answer for which setup to trade exclusively.
tasks.why: Phase 3 raises the bar from discipline to optimization.

variants: Add a 'Setup Variant' column to your trade journal — for each trade going forward, note which variation of your entry rule triggered it (e.g., 'breakout,' 'pullback,' 'gap fill').
Update your journal template: add a 'Setup Type' tag to every trade row — you'll use this to track which variation of your entry rule performs best.
In your journal, add a field called 'Setup Variant' or 'Setup Sub-Type' — label each trade with the specific trigger pattern that fired (whatever sub-categories fit your style).
tasks.why: Tagging setup variants is the first step to finding which one is your true edge.

variants: Write down 2-3 variations of your primary setup that you've noticed yourself taking — give each one a name and a one-sentence description of how they differ.
Identify the sub-types of your primary setup: list them by name and write one sentence explaining what distinguishes each from the others.
Define your setup variants in your journal: name each one, describe the specific chart condition that triggers it, and note which you take most often.
tasks.why: Named variants can be tracked — unnamed variants stay invisible.

variants: Go back to your Phase 2 trade log and retroactively tag each trade with a setup variant — then count how many of each variant you took and what the win rate was per variant.
Retroactively label every Phase 2 trade with its setup sub-type and build a quick summary: how many trades per variant and the win rate for each.
Tag all your Phase 2 trades with the appropriate setup variant, then compute a simple table: variant name, trade count, and win % — write it in your journal.
tasks.why: Even a rough retroactive tagging reveals which variant is most reliable.

variants: Pre-market plan with setup variant tagging active — which variants are you watching for today, and which specific tickers show each one.
Write today's plan and note which setup variant you expect to trigger on each watchlist ticker.
Pre-session plan: list tickers, triggers, and for each one note which variant of your setup it represents.
tasks.why: Pre-labeling expected variant keeps you objective about what you're actually seeing.

variants: Pull up the Day 31 variant win-rate table and identify which variant has the best win rate — write that variant's name at the top of your journal as 'Target Variant.'
From your Day 31 retroactive tag summary, pick the variant with the highest win % and call it your 'Primary Candidate' — write it in bold at the top of your journal.
Based on your variant performance data from yesterday, identify the single best-performing variant so far and label it your 'Focus Variant' for Phase 3.
tasks.why: Naming the best-performing variant makes the optimization target concrete.

variants: Log today's trades with setup variant tags, check that each entry matched your pre-labeled watchlist plan, and update your running variant performance table.
Post-session: fill in all trade fields including setup variant, then add today's trades to your variant stats table — update win rate per variant.
Complete your daily log with variant tags, then update the variant performance table with today's new data — what changed in the win rates?
tasks.why: The variant table only becomes useful when it's updated every day.

variants: Pre-market plan with today's vulnerability, Phase 2 constraints, and variant tag ready — which variant do you expect to see triggered today.
Write your full pre-session plan including Phase 2 rules, today's watchlist with variant pre-labels, and the vulnerability line.
Pre-session plan: Phase 2 limits, watchlist, variant pre-labels for each setup — write it all before the open.
tasks.why: Full protocol maintained — nothing drops off as new elements are added.

variants: Calculate your realized R:R for each setup variant separately using your Phase 2 data — add a 'Avg R:R' column to your variant table.
Add average realized R:R to your variant performance table: for each variant, compute the average of (actual gain / initial risk) across all trades.
Expand your variant stats table with R:R data: for each variant, calculate average realized R:R and add it as a column — variants with high win rate but poor R:R are less valuable.
tasks.why: Win rate alone is misleading without R:R — some setups win often but lose big.

variants: Log today's trades, update the variant table, and write a one-paragraph analysis of which variant looks best when both win rate and R:R are considered together.
Post-session: fill in the log, update variant stats with today's data, then write 2-3 sentences on which variant has the best combined profile (win rate + R:R).
Complete the daily log, update the variant performance table, and write one paragraph identifying which setup variant looks strongest on the combined metrics — win rate, R:R, and trade count.
tasks.why: Combining win rate and R:R gives a truer picture of a setup's edge.

variants: Pre-market plan — today, specifically look for your Target Variant from Day 32 and see if it's setting up on any tickers.
Write your pre-session plan with a focus on your best-performing variant: which tickers show it, and what's the exact trigger.
Pre-market plan with a specific search for your Focus Variant setups — list any tickers where it's forming and plan them fully.
tasks.why: Actively seeking the best variant builds recognition for it.

variants: Pull up 5 tickers and scan specifically for your best-performing setup variant — add any clean examples to your watchlist with full plans.
Spend 4 minutes scanning for your Target Variant across your usual watchlist — how many setups show it clearly today?
Scan your universe for the specific variant identified as your best — list every ticker where it's forming and note the quality of each setup.
tasks.why: Focused scanning trains you to find the setup you're actually good at.

variants: Log today's trades, tag variants, update the table, and write your observation on how often your best variant sets up in a typical day — was today's scan above or below average frequency.
Post-session: complete the log, update variant stats, and write a sentence on the supply of your best variant: how often does it appear on a normal trading day.
Fill in the daily log, update the variant performance table, and write a brief note on the frequency of your best-performing variant — does it appear enough to build a strategy around.
tasks.why: Setup frequency matters: a great variant that rarely appears isn't a primary strategy.

variants: Review your journal from Days 28-34 — all logs, variant tables, and analysis — before writing your weekly review.
Read Days 28-34 in full before your Week 5 review: trade logs, variant stats, analysis paragraphs — all of it.
Open your journal and read the past 7 days before writing anything new — trade data, variant updates, post-mortems included.
tasks.why: Weekly reviews require fresh engagement with the actual data.

variants: Write your Week 5 Review: total trades, in-plan %, exit discipline %, variant breakdown (count and win rate per variant), and which variant you're now calling your Primary.
Week 5 stats: trades taken, plan adherence %, exit compliance %, win rate and R:R per variant — and a one-sentence declaration of which variant you're prioritizing.
Create your Week 5 journal entry: phase metrics (in-plan %, exit %, R:R), variant performance table updated, and a decision: which variant is your primary going into the next 2 weeks.
tasks.why: Week 5 is decision point — the variant data is now sufficient to act on.

variants: Write a binding rule in your journal: starting Day 36, you will prioritize your declared Primary Variant and de-prioritize others — write the specific criteria for what would make you take a non-primary variant trade.
Commit in writing to your Primary Variant for Phase 3: write the rule for when you can take other variants (strict criteria only) and when you will pass.
Add a 'Primary Variant Rule' to your trading rules doc: your declared best variant, the specific conditions required to take a different variant, and what 'de-prioritize' means in practice.
tasks.why: A written rule about which variant to trade prevents drift back to scattered execution.

variants: Pre-market plan with Primary Variant search first — scan for your best variant before anything else and build the watchlist around it.
Write today's plan starting with a scan for your Primary Variant: which tickers show it today, then add any secondary candidates.
Pre-session plan: look for your Primary Variant first, plan those setups fully, then consider secondary variants only if primary isn't setting up clearly.
tasks.why: Primary Variant first is now the protocol.

variants: Find at least 2 tickers showing your Primary Variant today and plan them with entry, stop, target, and R:R pre-calculated.
Identify 2 Primary Variant setups from today's market and write a full trade plan for each: trigger, stop, target, R:R.
Scan for your Primary Variant and build complete plans for the 2 best examples: entry trigger, stop level, target, and calculated R:R for each.
tasks.why: Two fully planned setups is enough for a disciplined session.

variants: Log today's trades with all fields, tag each with variant type, update your variant table, and note whether you passed on any non-primary setups you would have taken before Week 5.
Post-session: complete the trade log, update variant stats, and write one sentence on whether you declined any non-primary setups today — if yes, how did it feel.
Fill in the daily log, update variant performance, and write a brief note on your selectivity today: did you stick to Primary Variant priority, and was it hard to pass on other setups.
tasks.why: Tracking selectivity decisions builds the habit of being choosy.

variants: Pre-market plan: Primary Variant scan first, then secondary, then write today's setups with all fields complete before the open.
Write your pre-session plan, prioritizing Primary Variant setups — if none are showing, write that explicitly before adding any other setups.
Start your plan by scanning for Primary Variant: if you find clean examples, plan them; if not, note 'No Primary today' and decide whether to watch secondary or sit out.
tasks.why: Writing 'No Primary today' is a valid and disciplined plan.

variants: Pull up two of your best IN-PLAN Primary Variant trades from Phase 2 and re-annotate them in TradingView — save fresh screenshots with today's date.
Open TradingView, find your two cleanest Primary Variant executions from Phase 2, and re-draw the entry/stop/target on each — save as updated reference charts.
Find two exemplar Primary Variant trades from your Phase 2 log, open them in TradingView, and annotate or re-annotate the key levels — save as 'PrimaryRef-[1/2]-Day37.png.'
tasks.why: Updating your reference charts keeps the visual template current.

variants: Log today's trades, update the variant table, and compare today's Primary Variant setups to your new reference screenshots — how close was the actual pattern to the reference.
Post-session: fill in the log, update stats, and write a one-sentence comparison of today's setups to your Day 37 reference charts — are you entering cleaner or messier setups.
Complete the daily log, update variant performance, and write a brief quality assessment: did today's actual entries match the reference pattern quality or were they lower-grade setups.
tasks.why: Comparing live entries to reference charts builds quality standards for entry.

variants: Pre-market plan: Primary Variant first, full protocol, vulnerability line — 2 minutes, no more.
Write your pre-session plan: Phase 2 constraints, Primary Variant priority, today's setups — then open the market.
Plan first, trade second — pre-session entry this morning covers Phase 2 rules, Primary Variant note, and today's watchlist.
tasks.why: The routine is locked in — execute it fast and completely.

variants: Run a paper trade on a setup you're uncertain about today — trade it on paper with full plan and track the result, but don't go real-money on it.
If a setup today feels 'maybe good enough' but not clearly a Primary Variant, paper trade it instead of going live — log it as paper and track outcome.
Identify one borderline setup today and paper trade it: write the full plan, track it, and log the result — you'll use this data to calibrate your selectivity.
tasks.why: Paper trading uncertain setups keeps real money reserved for high-confidence entries.

variants: Log today's real and paper trades, compare their outcomes, and write one sentence on whether the borderline setup you paper-traded should be in your plan or excluded.
Post-session: fill in real and paper trade logs, compare results, and make a judgment call: does the borderline setup qualify as a real variant or should it stay paper-only.
Complete all trade logs (real + paper), then write a verdict on your paper trade: does it meet the bar for your Primary Variant, a secondary variant, or should it be permanently excluded.
tasks.why: Paper trades convert uncertain setups into data-driven decisions.

variants: Pre-market plan, Primary Variant priority, plus one new element: write a 'Session Intention' line — one sentence on what discipline behavior you're specifically focusing on today.
Write your pre-session plan and add a Session Intention line: 'Today I am focused on [specific behavior]' — make it about one thing, not a list.
Complete your pre-session plan and write your Session Intention: a single focus behavior for today's session — exit discipline, not taking non-primary setups, or sticking to size limits.
tasks.why: A single session intention focuses improvement on one variable at a time.

variants: In your journal, add a 'Week 6 Performance Table' with columns for each trading day: in-plan %, exit discipline %, variant quality score (1-3), and total day P&L.
Create a Week 6 daily tracking table in your journal: Day number, in-plan %, exit discipline %, Primary Variant % of trades, and P&L — fill in today's row after the session.
Build a week-level performance grid in your journal: one row per trading day with columns for plan adherence, exit discipline, variant mix, and P&L — start filling it in from Day 36.
tasks.why: A weekly table lets you see trends within the week, not just across weeks.

variants: Log today's trades, update all tables, and write a one-sentence evaluation of your Session Intention — did the single focus improve that metric today vs your recent average.
Post-session: fill in the trade log, update the Week 6 table, and evaluate your Session Intention: did focusing on one behavior move the needle.
Complete the daily log, fill in the Week 6 performance row, and write a one-sentence verdict on your Session Intention — is this a behavior worth focusing on for more than one day.
tasks.why: Evaluating the session intention daily is what turns it from a feel-good exercise into a feedback loop.

variants: Pre-market plan: Session Intention, Phase 2 constraints, Primary Variant scan, watchlist — complete before the open.
Write your full pre-session plan including today's Session Intention — make it specific to one behavior you're still working to improve.
Pre-session: note your Session Intention first, then Phase 2 rules, then your Primary Variant watchlist — all three sections required.
tasks.why: The Session Intention becomes part of the standard pre-session protocol.

variants: Look at your variant performance table and compare your Primary Variant's win rate now vs your Day 31 retroactive baseline — write the delta.
Pull up your variant stats table and calculate how much the Primary Variant win rate has changed since you started actively prioritizing it on Day 36.
Check whether your Primary Variant performance has improved, declined, or stayed flat since you started focusing on it — calculate the before/after win rate.
tasks.why: Tracking whether focus on a variant improves its results validates the strategy.

variants: Log today's trades, update the variant table and Week 6 grid, and write one paragraph on whether you believe in your Primary Variant more or less than you did on Day 32 — cite specific trade outcomes.
Post-session: complete the log, update all tracking tables, and write a conviction assessment: has the data over the past week increased or decreased your confidence in the Primary Variant.
Fill in the daily log, update variant and week tables, then write 2-3 sentences on your current conviction level in the Primary Variant — more confident, less confident, or unchanged.
tasks.why: Conviction should be evidence-based, not emotional — write it down.

variants: Pre-market plan, Session Intention focused on the specific metric most improved since Day 31, and Primary Variant watchlist.
Write your pre-session plan with a Session Intention that targets your current weakest metric from the Week 6 table.
Pre-session: Session Intention targeting your lowest-performing metric from the last 5 days, Phase 2 constraints, Primary Variant priority, and today's setups.
tasks.why: Targeting the weakest metric accelerates overall improvement.

variants: Run a paper trade alongside your real trades today — find the best non-Primary Variant setup and paper-trade it while trading your Primary Variant live.
Identify the best setup that doesn't qualify as your Primary Variant and paper-trade it: full plan, full tracking, result logged — real money only on Primary.
Paper-trade one non-primary setup today: write the plan, track the entry, record the exit — compare it to how your Primary Variant setups performed.
tasks.why: Continuing to paper-trade non-primary setups keeps data flowing on alternative variants.

variants: Log real trades and paper trade, update all tables, and write your observation: are your non-primary paper trades performing comparably to or worse than your Primary Variant.
Post-session: fill in all logs, update the week grid, and write one sentence on whether your paper trades are outperforming, matching, or underperforming your Primary Variant.
Complete the daily log (real + paper), update all tracking tables, and write a data-based conclusion: is your Primary Variant still the right choice, or is a different variant challenging it.
tasks.why: Ongoing paper-trading validates the Primary Variant decision with live market data.

variants: Read Days 35-41 in full before writing your Week 6 review — trade logs, variant tables, Week 6 grid, session intentions, paper trade results — all of it.
Before your 6-week review, read everything from Day 35 through yesterday: logs, variant data, performance grid, paper trades — full read first.
Open your journal and read Days 35-41 completely: all trade entries, variant updates, week table, and session intention evaluations — then start the review.
tasks.why: The 6-week review is a milestone that requires full context.

variants: Write your 6-Week Review: in-plan % trend (getting better or worse), exit discipline % trend, Primary Variant win rate vs other variants, paper trade results summary, and your current clean streak.
6-Week Report: plan adherence trend, exit discipline trend, Primary Variant performance vs secondary variants, paper trade outcomes, and one sentence on whether you're on track for Phase 4.
Create your 6-week stats entry: trend in plan adherence over 6 weeks, trend in exit discipline, Primary Variant win rate, non-primary paper trade comparison, and current streak.
tasks.why: Six weeks of trend data shows whether behavior is improving or plateauing.

variants: Write a 6-week binding commitment: state which variant is your Primary, commit to a specific in-plan % target for Days 43-49, and write the one session behavior you'll focus on improving.
Commit to your next week in writing: Primary Variant confirmed or updated, minimum in-plan % target, and one Session Intention you'll carry through the week.
6-week commitment entry: lock in your Primary Variant choice with evidence, set a minimum plan-adherence target for the next 7 days, and name the single behavior you're improving next.
tasks.why: Week 7 starts with a commitment, not a hope.

variants: Pre-market plan with your 6-week commitment targets visible — write the in-plan % target and Session Intention at the top before your setups.
Write your pre-session plan with this week's commitment targets at the top: in-plan % goal and the specific behavior you're focusing on.
Open your journal: write this week's targets first (in-plan % and Session Intention), then your Phase 2 rules, then today's Primary Variant watchlist.
tasks.why: Week-level targets at the top of each day's plan keep the bigger goal visible.

variants: Scan for Primary Variant setups with a raised quality bar: today you're only adding a ticker to the watchlist if the setup is an A or B quality — define what A/B means to you and write it.
Before building today's watchlist, write your quality grading criteria: what makes a Primary Variant setup an A (ideal), B (acceptable), or C (pass) — then only plan A and B setups.
Define your setup quality scale in your journal today: A = textbook setup, B = slightly imperfect but still valid, C = do not take — then grade every watchlist candidate.
tasks.why: A quality grading system raises the bar for entry without reducing it to a feeling.

variants: Log today's trades with setup quality grades added (A/B/C per trade), update all tables, and write whether higher quality grades correlated with better outcomes.
Post-session: fill in the trade log with quality grades for each entry, update variant stats, and write a one-sentence observation on whether A-grade setups outperformed B-grade.
Complete the daily log with quality grades included, update the week performance table, and analyze: did your A-graded entries beat your B-graded entries today — yes, no, or not enough data yet.
tasks.why: Quality grading per trade builds the data to justify higher selectivity.

variants: Pre-market plan with week targets, quality grades ready, Primary Variant priority — write it fast and completely.
Write the pre-session plan: week targets visible, Primary Variant scan, quality threshold (A/B only), today's specific setups.
Pre-session: week targets at top, Primary Variant watchlist with quality grades pre-assigned, Phase 2 constraints confirmed.
tasks.why: The plan is now layered with quality grading — build the habit of including it.

variants: In your journal, calculate your A-grade and B-grade win rates separately from all trades taken since you started quality grading on Day 43.
Build a quality-grade performance mini-table: A-grade trades (win rate, avg R:R), B-grade trades (win rate, avg R:R) — even with 1-2 days of data, write what you have.
From the trades since Day 43, compute win rate for A-grade entries vs B-grade entries and write both numbers — early data is still data.
tasks.why: Even 1-2 days of quality-graded data starts the calibration.

variants: Log today's trades with quality grades, update the grade performance table, and write whether your gut grade before entry has matched the actual outcome so far.
Post-session: fill in the log with quality grades, update the A/B performance table, and write one sentence on whether your grading instinct appears to be calibrated.
Complete the daily log with grade tags, update the quality performance mini-table, and write a brief observation: is A-grade doing better than B-grade in your early data.
tasks.why: Calibrating the grade instinct is an iterative process.

variants: Pre-market plan with week targets, quality grades, Primary Variant — and today write your vulnerability line specifically around grade discipline: what would make you take a C-grade setup.
Write your pre-session plan including your grade-discipline vulnerability: what specific pressure or FOMO would tempt you to enter a C-grade setup today.
Pre-session plan: all standard elements plus a grade-discipline vulnerability line — write the scenario most likely to make you lower your quality bar and your planned response.
tasks.why: Anticipating grade compression is the next layer of discipline pre-commitment.

variants: Go back to your trade log and find the last 5 C-grade or IMPULSE trades you took — calculate their combined P&L and write the total in your journal.
Look up your 5 lowest-grade or most impulsive entries from Phase 2 and Phase 3 so far — add up the P&L on all 5 and write what they cost you.
In your journal, identify your worst 5 trade decisions since Day 15 (C-grade or impulse), find the P&L for each in your brokerage, and total the damage.
tasks.why: Calculating the cost of low-grade trades turns an abstract concept into a dollar amount.

variants: Log today's trades with quality grades, update all tables, and write a one-paragraph reflection on the dollar cost you calculated and what eliminating C-grade trades would mean for your monthly P&L.
Post-session: complete the log, update tables, and write 3 sentences on the cost of your worst 5 trades — what would your P&L look like if those trades simply didn't happen.
Fill in the daily log and write a reflection: based on the dollar cost from your 5 worst decisions, what specific monthly P&L improvement would come from eliminating trades below A/B grade.
tasks.why: Quantifying the cost of bad trades is more motivating than abstract discipline talk.

variants: Write today's pre-market plan and add a new section: 'This Week's P&L Review Protocol' — you'll conduct a full weekly P&L review every Friday starting this week.
Pre-session plan with a new element: this week you start weekly P&L reviews — write the day and time you'll do it and what the review will cover.
Write your pre-session plan and commit to a weekly P&L review ritual: define the day, time, and format — this is a non-negotiable weekly event starting this week.
tasks.why: Weekly P&L reviews are the cornerstone of Phase 4 — establish the ritual now.

variants: Create your Weekly P&L Review template in your journal: sections for total P&L, in-plan %, exit discipline %, quality grade breakdown, best trade of the week, worst trade of the week, and next-week focus.
Build the format for your weekly review: total P&L, plan adherence %, exit discipline %, A/B/C grade trade breakdown, top trade, worst trade, next week's primary setup focus.
Design your Weekly Review template with these sections: week P&L, in-plan %, exit %, grade breakdown (A/B/C counts), one winner to repeat, one mistake to eliminate, and next-week intent.
tasks.why: A structured template makes weekly reviews fast and consistent.

variants: Log today's trades, update all tables, then write a preview of what your weekly review will look like based on this week's data — a dry run before the full review day.
Post-session: fill in the trade log and tables, then draft a partial weekly summary for this week so far — get comfortable with the format before the first full review.
Complete the daily log, update all tracking tables, and write a partial weekly summary entry: fill in what you know so far for the Week P&L, in-plan %, and grade breakdown.
tasks.why: A practice run makes the actual weekly review faster and more accurate.

variants: Pre-market plan: Primary Variant priority, quality grade threshold (A/B only), Session Intention, week targets — all written before the open.
Write your full pre-session plan: week targets visible, A/B grade only policy, Primary Variant watchlist, today's Session Intention.
Pre-session plan with all layers: week-level targets at top, Phase 2 constraints, quality grade policy, Primary Variant watchlist.
tasks.why: All discipline layers active simultaneously — this is the standard.

variants: Scan for your Primary Variant setups, grade each one A/B/C, and add only A and B setups to your watchlist with complete plans.
Build your watchlist with quality filtering: find Primary Variant candidates, grade them, and exclude any C-grade entries — only plan what you'd actually trade.
