Find Primary Variant setups, assign quality grades, and build your watchlist from A and B grades only — note how many C-grades you filtered out.
tasks.why: Quality-filtered watchlists reduce in-session decision fatigue.

variants: Log today's trades, update variant and quality-grade tables, and check whether the number of C-grade setups you filtered out pre-market saved you from any losses.
Post-session: complete the log, update all tables, and write a one-sentence note on the value of your pre-market quality filter — did anything you passed on play out badly.
Fill in the daily log, update tracking tables, and evaluate your quality filter: of the C-grade setups you excluded from the watchlist, how did they actually play out.
tasks.why: Validating the quality filter retroactively builds confidence in stricter selectivity.

variants: Pre-market plan with all standard elements — today's Session Intention should specifically address your next-week planning: what are you going to focus on improving next week.
Write your pre-session plan and include a next-week intention line: what one thing will you do better in the week after this one.
Complete your pre-session plan and add a forward-looking note: based on this week's data so far, what is the one thing you want to prioritize next week.
tasks.why: Thinking ahead one week creates continuity between review periods.

variants: Pull up your last 15 trades and sort them by setup quality grade — calculate win rate and average R:R for A-grade, B-grade, and any C-grade trades separately.
From the past 15 entries in your trade log, compute three performance profiles: A-grade trades (win rate + R:R), B-grade (win rate + R:R), C-grade if any (win rate + R:R).
Build a 15-trade quality grade analysis: sort your last 15 logged trades by grade and compute win rate and average R:R for each grade — write all six numbers.
tasks.why: Fifteen trades is enough data to see a quality-grade performance pattern.

variants: Log today's trades, update all tables, and write a one-paragraph analysis of the quality-grade data you just computed — does taking only A and B setups improve your P&L expectancy.
Post-session: fill in the log, update tables, and write your quality-grade conclusion: is there a meaningful performance gap between A and B and C grade trades in your 15-trade sample.
Complete the daily log and write a brief analysis of your quality-grade split: does the data support being highly selective, or are B-grade setups performing just as well as A-grade ones.
tasks.why: Data-driven quality standards are more sustainable than arbitrary rules.

variants: Read Days 42-48 before writing your Week 7 review — all logs, variant tables, quality grade tables, session intentions — full read.
Before the week 7 review, read all journal entries from Day 42 through yesterday: trade data, grade tables, session intention evaluations — complete read.
Open your journal and read Days 42-48 in full: trade logs, variant performance, quality grade breakdown, week table — all of it before writing.
tasks.why: The weekly review requires fresh engagement with actual data from the period.

variants: Conduct your Week 7 P&L Review using your template: fill in total P&L, in-plan %, exit discipline %, grade breakdown (A/B/C counts and P&L per grade), best and worst trades of the week.
Run your weekly P&L review now: total week P&L, plan adherence %, exit discipline %, quality grade split by count and P&L, one trade to repeat and one to eliminate.
Complete your first full Weekly P&L Review using the template from Day 46: all sections filled in — total P&L, adherence %, exit %, grade breakdown, best trade, worst trade.
tasks.why: The first full weekly review sets the standard for all future reviews.

variants: Write your next-week plan based on the weekly review: which Primary Variant setups to prioritize, what quality grade threshold to enforce, and one specific behavior to improve — with a numeric target.
Based on your Week 7 review, write Week 8's plan: confirm Primary Variant, set minimum A-grade % of total trades, and define the specific metric you're improving with a target number.
Use your Week 7 review to set Week 8 standards: Primary Variant confirmed, quality grade minimum policy stated, and one improvement metric with a specific target — write it as a commitment.
tasks.why: The weekly review is only valuable if it produces a concrete next-week plan.

variants: Pre-market plan with Week 8 targets visible — quality grade minimum, Primary Variant priority, session intention — all at the top before your setups.
Write your Week 8 pre-session plan: week targets at the top, then phase constraints, then Primary Variant watchlist with quality grades pre-assigned.
Start Week 8's pre-session plan with your week-level standards: grade threshold, Primary Variant priority, and Session Intention — then add today's specific setups.
tasks.why: Week 8 starts with week-level targets set from the review — apply them immediately.

variants: Look at your equity curve from Day 17's screenshot and compare it to your current P&L chart — screenshot today's and add it to your journal with a one-sentence comment on the trend.
Pull up today's P&L or equity curve from your brokerage, screenshot it, and add it to your journal next to the Day 17 snapshot — write one sentence comparing the two.
Screenshot your current P&L chart from your brokerage and drop it in your journal — compare it to your Day 17 equity curve screenshot and write what you see.
tasks.why: Periodic equity curve comparisons show whether the discipline work is showing up in money.

variants: Log today's trades with all fields, update variant and quality-grade tables, and write a one-paragraph note on how the equity curve comparison made you feel — and whether the feeling matches the data.
Post-session: complete the trade log, update all tracking tables, and write a brief journal note on the equity curve comparison — is the trend improving, and does your data explain why.
Fill in the daily log, update performance tables, and write 2-3 sentences on the equity curve comparison: is discipline translating into better P&L trends, or is the curve still flat?
tasks.why: Connecting discipline metrics to the equity curve creates a feedback loop that actually matters.

variants: Pre-market plan — all protocol, all layers — written in under 2 minutes because the habit is locked in.
Write your complete pre-session plan: week targets, Primary Variant watchlist with grades, Session Intention, Phase 2 constraints — fast and complete.
Pre-session plan written: constraints, week targets, quality-graded watchlist, Session Intention — all of it before the open.
tasks.why: By now the pre-session plan should be a fast, complete ritual.

variants: Find the reference chart you saved on Day 37 and compare it to today's best Primary Variant setup — write one sentence on whether the current setup matches reference quality.
Pull up your Day 37 Primary Variant reference screenshots and find a current chart that mirrors one of them — write whether it qualifies as A-grade based on the reference.
Open the reference charts from Day 37 and scan for a current setup that matches the same structure — grade it and add it to your watchlist if it qualifies.
tasks.why: Using reference charts as a grading tool makes quality assessment objective.

variants: Log today's trades, update tables, and write a one-sentence observation on whether the reference-chart comparison helped you grade setups more accurately today.
Post-session: fill in the log, update tracking tables, and write a brief note on using the reference charts as quality filters — did it help you make better entry decisions.
Complete the daily log, update all tables, and write one sentence on whether the reference-chart grading made your quality assessment easier or harder today.
tasks.why: Iterating on grading tools makes the quality assessment process more reliable.

variants: Pre-market plan with all layers — today add a specific entry: 'If I cannot find an A or B grade Primary Variant setup, I will not trade today.' Write it explicitly.
Write your pre-session plan and include the sit-out rule: 'If no A/B grade Primary Variant appears today, I will sit out and paper trade any borderline setups instead.'
Complete your pre-session plan and write the sit-out condition: explicitly state what has to be true for you to decide today is a no-trade day.
tasks.why: Writing an explicit sit-out condition makes not trading a decision, not a failure.

variants: Scan for Primary Variant setups and grade them — if nothing is A or B, write 'No Trade Today' in your journal and paper trade one borderline setup instead.
Go through your usual scan: if you find A/B grade Primary setups, plan them; if you don't, invoke the sit-out rule — write the outcome in your journal either way.
Run your pre-market scan with quality grading: if setups grade out as A or B, add them to your watchlist; if not, write 'Sit Out' in today's journal entry and find a paper trade instead.
tasks.why: Executing the sit-out rule is a discipline win equal to a good trade.

variants: After the session, log what you traded or write 'Sat Out' in your log — include why you made the call and what the market looked like at the time.
Post-session: log your trades or log your sit-out decision — if you sat out, write what you saw, why it didn't qualify, and whether the decision was validated by how the session played out.
Complete today's log whether you traded or sat out — if you passed on the market today, write what you would have traded, what grade it was, and what actually happened with it.
tasks.why: Logging sit-out decisions the same as trades treats discipline as a tradeable asset.

variants: Pre-market plan with all standard layers — and the sit-out rule is now a standing policy, written at the top of today's entry.
Write your pre-session plan with the sit-out policy stated as a standing rule: 'No A/B grade Primary = No live trade.' It applies every day from now on.
Pre-session plan: week targets, Primary Variant with quality grading, sit-out rule stated — all before the open.
tasks.why: The sit-out rule becomes permanent policy, not a one-day experiment.

variants: In your journal, calculate how many trading days in the past 4 weeks you would have sat out if the A/B-only policy had been in place — estimate using your quality-grade historical data.
Look back at your Phase 3 trade log and estimate: on how many days did you take only C-grade or impulse trades — those would now be sit-out days under the new policy.
Retroactively apply the sit-out rule to your Phase 3 data: count the days where all trades were C-grade or impulse — how many are now sit-out days in hindsight.
tasks.why: Retroactive application shows the policy's impact before you have live sit-out data.

variants: Log today's session (trade or sit-out), update all tables, and write a one-paragraph reflection on what sitting out feels like vs what a C-grade loss feels like — compare the discomfort level.
Post-session: fill in the log, update tracking tables, and write 2-3 sentences comparing the emotional experience of a disciplined sit-out vs the regret of taking a bad trade.

Complete the daily log and write a brief journal entry on the psychology of sitting out: is not trading harder or easier than you expected, and how does it compare to the feeling after a losing impulse trade.
tasks.why: Sitting out feels wrong before the habit forms — writing about it accelerates the reframe.

variants: Pre-market plan — full protocol including sit-out rule — then write what a perfect trading day looks like for you right now: specific setup, specific execution, specific exit.
Write your pre-session plan and add a 'Perfect Day Template': describe your ideal session from pre-scan through journal entry in 3-4 sentences.
Complete pre-session plan plus a brief definition of a perfect trading day: what would the pre-market scan, the entry, the management, and the exit all look like at your current standards.
tasks.why: Defining a perfect day creates a template to measure actual sessions against.

variants: Take the perfect day definition you just wrote and compare it against your 3 best recent sessions — score each one out of 10 on how close it came to perfect.
Pull up your 3 best trading days from Phase 3 and score each against your Perfect Day Template: entry quality, exit discipline, risk compliance — assign a score out of 10.
Grade your last 3 'best' sessions against the Perfect Day Template: how close were they on entry quality, exit discipline, and position sizing — write a score and 1-sentence note per day.
tasks.why: Scoring sessions against an ideal creates a performance ladder.

variants: Log today's session, score it against your Perfect Day Template, update all tables, and write one sentence on what would need to change for today to score higher.
Post-session: fill in the log, score today's session against the Perfect Day Template, update tracking tables, and write the single change that would move the score up by 2 points.
Complete the daily log with a Perfect Day score for today's session, update all tables, and write a brief note on the one thing keeping you from a higher score.
tasks.why: Daily scoring against the template shows where the gap between good and ideal consistently lives.

variants: Pre-market plan — all protocol — and today, write your Perfect Day score from yesterday at the top: your target is to beat it by at least 1 point today.
Write your pre-session plan with yesterday's Perfect Day score visible and a target to beat it by at least one point today.
Pre-session plan with yesterday's Perfect Day score at the top: you're chasing a higher score today — write what specifically you'll do differently to earn it.
tasks.why: Day-over-day score improvement creates a concrete performance target.

variants: Review your Phase 3 data and calculate your average Perfect Day score so far (even if only 2 days) — write the average and what's pulling it down most.
From your Day 54 and today's scores, write your current average Perfect Day score and identify the one dimension that consistently pulls the score down.
Look at your Perfect Day scores so far and identify the lowest-scoring dimension across sessions: is it entry quality, exit discipline, or risk compliance — write the answer.
tasks.why: The lowest-scoring dimension is the next improvement target.

variants: Log today's session, score it, update all tables, and write a one-paragraph comparison: did you beat yesterday's score, and what specifically drove the result.
Post-session: complete the log, score today vs yesterday, update tracking tables, and write a brief analysis of what you did differently today and whether it moved the score.
Fill in the daily log, assign today's Perfect Day score, update all tables, and write 2 sentences: what you changed from yesterday and whether it improved the score.
tasks.why: Day-to-day comparison is the tightest improvement feedback loop.

variants: Read Days 49-55 in full before writing your Week 8 review — all logs, quality grade tables, Perfect Day scores, weekly review — complete read first.
Before your week 8 review, read everything from Day 49 through yesterday: trade logs, variant and quality tables, Perfect Day scores — full read.
Open your journal and read Days 49-55: trade data, all tables updated, Perfect Day scores, session intention evaluations — read it all.
tasks.why: The 8-week review needs full context before writing.

variants: Run your Week 8 P&L Review using your template: total P&L, in-plan %, exit discipline %, grade breakdown, average Perfect Day score, best and worst trades, next-week priority.
Complete the Week 8 review: P&L, plan adherence %, exit %, quality grade split, average Perfect Day score, one trade to repeat, one to eliminate, next-week focus.
Week 8 formal review: fill in all template sections including P&L, in-plan %, exit %, grade breakdown, Perfect Day score average, best trade, worst trade, and Week 9 intent.
tasks.why: The weekly review ritual is now a mature system — fill it completely.

variants: Write your Week 9 plan based on the review: Primary Variant confirmed, quality grade policy, Perfect Day score target, one metric to improve with a specific number.
Based on your Week 8 review, write Week 9's commitments: Primary Variant, A/B grade minimum, target Perfect Day score, and the single metric you're improving.
Use the Week 8 review to set Week 9 standards: lock in your Primary Variant, set your quality grade floor, commit to a Perfect Day target score, and name your improvement metric.
tasks.why: Each weekly review produces a concrete next-week plan — the system is running.

variants: Pre-market plan with Week 9 targets at the top — write it fast, write it complete, then open the market.
Write today's pre-session plan with this week's targets and standards at the top — full protocol, no shortcuts.
Pre-session plan: Week 9 targets, sit-out rule, Primary Variant scan, quality grade threshold, Session Intention.
tasks.why: Week 9 standards are higher — apply them from the first session.

variants: Scan for Primary Variant setups, grade each one, and annotate 2 A-grade charts in TradingView — save them as today's reference examples.
Find 2 A-grade Primary Variant setups today and annotate them fully in TradingView (entry, stop, target) — save as fresh reference charts regardless of whether you trade them.
Identify the 2 cleanest Primary Variant setups you can find today, mark the levels on TradingView, and save as annotated references — these expand your reference library.
tasks.why: Building the reference chart library over time sharpens pattern recognition.

variants: Log today's trades (or sit-out), score the session on the Perfect Day scale, update all tables, and write one sentence on your Week 9 improvement metric for today.
Post-session: fill in the log, score the Perfect Day, update tracking tables, and note whether today moved your Week 9 improvement metric in the right direction.
Complete the daily log, assign Perfect Day score, update tables, and write a one-sentence update on your Week 9 focus metric — better, same, or worse than your baseline.
tasks.why: Daily tracking of the Week 9 metric starts the improvement cycle.

variants: Pre-market plan — Week 9 targets visible, full protocol — and today write your vulnerability line around the sit-out rule: what would make you trade on a no-setup day.
Write your pre-session plan and include a sit-out vulnerability line: what specific market condition or emotion would tempt you to trade when there's no A/B grade setup.
Complete your pre-session plan with a sit-out vulnerability pre-commitment: write the scenario most likely to make you override the sit-out rule and your planned response.
tasks.why: The sit-out rule will be tested — anticipating the test is the best defense.

variants: In your journal, calculate your current sit-out rate: out of all days since implementing the sit-out rule, how many were actual sit-out days — write the percentage.
Count your sit-out days since Day 52 and calculate what % of sessions resulted in no live trade — write the number and whether it feels too high, too low, or about right.
From Day 52 through today, count how many days you sat out vs traded — write the ratio and compare it to your pre-sit-out-rule frequency of low-grade trades.
tasks.why: Measuring sit-out frequency shows whether selectivity is being applied.

variants: Log today's session, score it on the Perfect Day scale, update tables, and write a one-paragraph reflection on whether your sit-out rate feels like discipline or avoidance.
Post-session: fill in the log, score the Perfect Day, update tracking tables, and write 2-3 sentences on whether your sit-out rate suggests healthy selectivity or a fear of trading.
Complete the daily log, assign the Perfect Day score, update tables, and write a brief journal note on the sit-out rate: is the frequency appropriate for your setup's natural appearance rate.
tasks.why: Distinguishing discipline from avoidance is a critical self-awareness skill.

variants: Pre-market plan with all Week 9 elements, then write a 'Setup Clarity Statement': in one sentence, describe your Primary Variant so precisely that someone else could identify it.
Write your pre-session plan and add a Setup Clarity exercise: define your Primary Variant in one sentence with enough specificity that it's testable, not vague.
Complete your pre-session plan and write a one-sentence definition of your Primary Variant that a stranger could use to find the same setup on a chart — no vagueness allowed.
tasks.why: If you can't define the setup precisely, you can't execute it consistently.

variants: Compare your one-sentence Setup Clarity Statement to your original Day 1 setup definition — write what changed and whether the new version is more precise.
Put your Day 59 Setup Clarity Statement next to your Day 1 written setup and write 2 sentences on how your understanding of the setup has evolved over 60 days.
Open your Day 1 journal entry, read your original setup definition, and compare it to today's clarity statement — write the key difference in specificity.
tasks.why: Comparing Day 1 to Day 59 shows concrete evidence of development.

variants: Log today's trades, score the Perfect Day, update all tables, and write a one-paragraph summary of how your setup understanding has evolved since Day 1 — be specific about what changed.
Post-session: complete the log, score the Perfect Day, update tables, and write a brief evolution summary: what do you know about your Primary Variant now that you didn't know on Day 1.
Fill in the daily log, assign Perfect Day score, update tables, and write a 3-sentence reflection on the evolution of your primary setup from Day 1's rough definition to today's precise statement.
tasks.why: Articulating growth builds the confidence that comes from earned knowledge.

variants: Write your 60-Day Milestone entry: record your key metrics — in-plan %, exit discipline %, Primary Variant win rate, average Perfect Day score, current clean streak — and compare each to your Day 2 baseline.
60-day checkpoint: pull your four key Phase 3 metrics (plan adherence %, exit %, Primary Variant win rate, Perfect Day average) and compare them to your Day 2 audit numbers.
Day 60 milestone: write your current in-plan %, exit discipline %, Primary Variant performance stats, and Perfect Day average — then write the delta from Day 2 for each.
tasks.why: Day 60 is a major milestone — the numbers show 60 days of real work.

variants: Write a 60-day honest assessment in three sections: (1) what is definitively better since Day 1, (2) what is still not resolved, and (3) what your Primary Variant setup definition looks like now vs Day 1.
60-day journal entry with three paragraphs: what's improved (with data), what's still breaking down (be specific), and how your setup definition has evolved from Day 1 to today.
Write your 60-day self-assessment: paragraph 1 on what's working with data, paragraph 2 on what's still not fixed, paragraph 3 comparing your Day 1 setup definition to your Day 59 clarity statement.
